summarization.a: The line chart compares literature-reported etch-per-cycle (EPC) values for thermal HF/TMA processes with the SF₆ plasma/TMA process demonstrated in this work. Data from this study use in situ spectroscopic ellipsometry to measure the actual substrate temperature, whereas the literature values correspond to reported set-point temperatures. References include ->Cano (HF/TMA), Hennessy (HF/TMA), Lee (HF/TMA), Zywotko (HF/TMA).

a.question: List the two distinct chemical reactant combinations being compared in this study for atomic layer etching (ALE).
a.answer:
SF₆ plasma / TMA: The chemistry utilized in "This work" (Green square trace).

Thermal HF / TMA: The standard chemistry cited from literature sources (Cano, Hennessy, Lee, Zywotko).

a.question: In the temperature range of 200°C to 300°C, how does the Etch Per Cycle (EPC) performance of the proposed SF₆/TMA process compare to the majority of the HF/TMA literature data?
a.answer: The SF₆/TMA process exhibits a significantly higher etch rate compared to the HF/TMA processes. While the HF/TMA data (excluding Cano) generally remains below 1.5 Å/cycle, the SF₆/TMA process rapidly climbs from ~2.0 Å/cycle to >3.0 Å/cycle in this temperature window.

a.question: The SF₆ plasma/TMA process maintains a high, stable EPC (~3 Å/cycle) from 200°C to 300°C. What does this temperature independence imply about the nature of the rate-limiting surface reaction?
a.answer: It implies the etch rate is limited by a plasma-driven surface reaction with low activation energy, not by a thermally activated step like precursor decomposition or ligand desorption.

a.question: For integrating Al₂O₃ ALE into the fabrication of advanced 3D NAND or DRAM memory chips, why would the SF₆ plasma/TMA process (this work) be the preferred choice over the thermal HF/TMA routes?
a.answer: Modern memory chips contain temperature-sensitive materials (e.g., metals, organics) that cannot withstand the >275°C required for thermal HF/TMA etching. The SF₆ plasma/TMA process achieves the necessary etch rate at ~200°C, compatible with these thermal constraints. Furthermore, the wide temperature window (200-300°C) offers robust process control across a wafer, which is critical for the high-aspect-ratio structures in 3D memory.
